tier_2_handbook.definition: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma is a malignant neoplasm arising from the exocrine pancreas, predominantly from the ductal epithelial cells. The pancreas is a retroperitoneal organ derived from the foregut (specifically, the dorsal and ventral pancreatic buds that fuse during embryological development). Anatomically, the pancreas is divided into four regions: the head (including the uncinate process), neck, body, and tail. The head lies within the curve of the duodenum and contains the ampulla of Vater, where the pancreatic and bile ducts converge. The neck is a short segment anterior to the superior mesenteric vessels. The body extends leftward and the tail reaches the splenic hilum. Pancreatic adenocarcinoma typically originates in the head or uncinate process (approximately 70-80% of cases) and is characterized by early local invasion of peripancreatic fat, nerves, and major vascular structures (e.g., superior mesenteric artery/vein, portal vein, celiac axis), as well as early metastasis to regional lymph nodes, liver, and peritoneum. The tumor often presents with obstructive jaundice (for head lesions), weight loss, abdominal pain, and new-onset diabetes. Histologically, it is usually a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma with dense desmoplastic stroma. The disease is staged using the AJCC TNM system (8th edition), which incorporates tumor size, lymph node involvement, and distant metastasis. The clinical resectability status (resectable, borderline resectable, locally advanced, metastatic) is determined by multidisciplinary evaluation of high-quality cross-sectional imaging (pancreatic protocol CT or MRI) and its relationship to major peripancreatic vessels.

subtypes.name: Invasive Ductal Adenocarcinoma (Not Otherwise Specified)
subtypes.description: The most common type, accounting for >90% of exocrine pancreatic malignancies. It arises from the pancreatic ductal epithelium and is characterized by glandular differentiation, marked desmoplasia, and early perineural invasion.
subtypes.key_associations: Strong association with KRAS mutations (present in >90% of cases). Often presents with biliary obstruction (head lesions).

subtypes.name: Adenosquamous Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A rare variant containing both glandular and squamous cell components. The squamous component must comprise at least 30% of the tumor for diagnosis.
subtypes.key_associations: May present similarly to typical adenocarcinoma. Prognosis is often poor, similar to or worse than conventional adenocarcinoma.

subtypes.name: Colloid (Mucinous Noncystic) Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Characterized by abundant extracellular mucin pools containing malignant epithelial cells. Often associated with an intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN) precursor.
subtypes.key_associations: May have a better prognosis than conventional adenocarcinoma due to lower grade and later stage at presentation.

subtypes.name: Hepatoid Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A rare variant with histological features resembling hepatocellular carcinoma. It may produce alpha-fetoprotein (AFP).

subtypes.name: Medullary Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Characterized by large, poorly differentiated tumor cells with syncytial growth, pushing borders, and prominent lymphocytic infiltration. Often associated with microsatellite instability (MSI) and mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR).
subtypes.key_associations: Strong association with MSI/dMMR. May respond to immune checkpoint inhibitors.

subtypes.name: Undifferentiated (Anaplastic) Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A highly aggressive variant with absence of glandular differentiation. May contain giant cells or be an undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells.
subtypes.key_associations: Very poor prognosis. Often presents at advanced stage.

subtypes.name: Signet Ring Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Characterized by cells with intracellular mucin vacuoles displacing the nucleus to the periphery, creating a 'signet ring' appearance.
subtypes.key_associations: Aggressive clinical course, often diffuse infiltration.

subtypes.name: Micropapillary Carcinoma
subtypes.description: An aggressive variant with small, hollow papillary tufts lacking fibrovascular cores, often present within retraction spaces.
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with high rate of lymph node metastasis and poor prognosis.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular pathogenesis of pancreatic adenocarcinoma involves a stepwise accumulation of genetic and epigenetic alterations. The most common driver mutation is in the KRAS oncogene (present in >90% of cases), leading to constitutive activation of the RAS-MAPK signaling pathway, which promotes cell proliferation and survival. Inactivating mutations in tumor suppressor genes are also frequent: TP53 (>75% of cases), SMAD4 (DPC4, ~55%), and CDKN2A/p16 (~90% via deletion, mutation, or promoter methylation). The TGF-β/SMAD pathway is disrupted by SMAD4 loss, altering growth control and immune evasion. Other recurrently mutated genes include ARID1A, TGFBR2, and chromatin remodeling genes (e.g., MLL, MLL3, KMT2C). Homologous recombination repair genes, such as BRCA1, BRCA2, and PALB2, are mutated in approximately 5-7% of pancreatic adenocarcinomas, leading to genomic instability and sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapies and PARP inhibitors. Microsatellite instability (MSI) and mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR) occur in <2% of cases but are therapeutically relevant due to responsiveness to immune checkpoint inhibitors. Other actionable molecular alterations include NTRK gene fusions (<1%), BRAF V600E mutations (~1-2%), HER2 amplification (<5%), and RET fusions (<1%). These are identified via next-generation sequencing (NGS) and guide targeted therapy. Tumor mutational burden (TMB) is also assessed to predict immunotherapy response. The tumor microenvironment is characterized by a dense desmoplastic stroma, immunosuppressive cells, and hypovascularity, which contribute to chemoresistance.

epidemiology.incidence: In 2026, an estimated 67,530 new cases of pancreatic cancer will be diagnosed in the United States (American Cancer Society, CA Cancer J Clin 2023). Globally, pancreatic cancer is the 12th most common cancer, with approximately 495,000 new cases annually (GLOBOCAN 2020).
epidemiology.mortality: Approximately 52,740 deaths from pancreatic cancer are projected in the United States in 2026, making it the fourth leading cause of cancer-related death. The 5-year overall survival rate for all stages combined is approximately 12%.
epidemiology.trends: Incidence rates have been increasing over the past two decades, particularly among Black women, who have experienced the greatest increase compared to other demographic groups. Mortality rates have remained largely unchanged, reflecting the lack of effective early detection and treatments for advanced disease.
epidemiology.demographics: Pancreatic cancer incidence increases with age, with a median age at diagnosis of 70 years. Men have a slightly higher incidence than women (ratio approximately 1.2:1). Black individuals have a higher incidence and mortality compared to White individuals. Incidence also varies by geography, with higher rates in industrialized nations.

risk_factors.detail: Cigarette smoking is firmly linked to an increased risk of pancreatic cancer. The risk is proportional to the number of cigarettes smoked per day and duration of smoking. Approximately 20-25% of pancreatic cancers are attributable to smoking. Cessation of smoking reduces the risk over time.
risk_factors.strength: Strong association. Relative risk (RR) approximately 1.5-2.0 for current smokers compared to never-smokers (Bosetti et al., Ann Oncol 2012).

risk_factors.detail: Elevated body mass index (BMI) is associated with an increased risk, with a dose-response relationship. Higher BMI during early adulthood is linked to increased pancreatic cancer mortality. Low levels of physical activity also confer an increased risk.
risk_factors.strength: Meta-analysis shows a RR of 1.19 (95% CI 1.10-1.29) for overweight individuals (BMI 25-29.9) and 1.51 (95% CI 1.31-1.74) for obese individuals (BMI ≥30) compared to normal weight. Physical activity: RR 0.93 (95% CI 0.88-0.98) for high vs. low activity (Behrens et al., Eur J Epidemiol 2015).

risk_factors.detail: New-onset diabetes (within 3 years of pancreatic cancer diagnosis) may be a paraneoplastic syndrome caused by the cancer. Long-standing diabetes (≥5 years) is a risk factor, though the association weakens with longer duration, possibly due to treatment effects. Prediabetes (impaired fasting glucose) is also associated with increased risk.
risk_factors.strength: Meta-analysis of 44 studies shows that the strength of association decreases with duration of diabetes. Patients with pancreatic cancer and diabetes have worse overall survival (14.4 vs. 21.7 months, P<0.001).

risk_factors.detail: Chronic pancreatitis is a well-established risk factor, with a 7.2-fold increased risk. The risk is highest in the first 2 years after diagnosis but remains elevated for up to 10 years.
risk_factors.strength: Strong association. Odds ratio (OR) 7.2 (95% CI 2.3-22.8) in a pooled analysis (Bracci et al., Cancer Causes Control 2009).

risk_factors.detail: Germline mutations in several genes confer increased risk, including BRCA1, BRCA2, PALB2, CDKN2A, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, STK11, ATM, and TP53. The lifetime risk for carriers of BRCA2 mutations is approximately 3-5%, and for Lynch syndrome genes it is around 3-4%.
risk_factors.strength: Strong genetic predisposition. Genetic counseling and testing are recommended for all patients with pancreatic cancer.

risk_factors.detail: A first-degree relative with pancreatic cancer increases the risk. The risk is further elevated with multiple affected relatives or early age of onset.
risk_factors.strength: OR 2.0-3.0 for one first-degree relative; higher with more affected relatives.

risk_factors.detail: Heavy alcohol use (≥3 drinks per day) is associated with an increased risk, possibly mediated through pancreatitis.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate association. RR 1.22 (95% CI 1.13-1.32) for high consumption (Bagnardi et al., Br J Cancer 2015).

risk_factors.detail: Periodontal disease is associated with an increased risk, even after adjusting for other risk factors such as smoking and BMI.
risk_factors.strength: Meta-analysis shows a summary RR of 1.64 (95% CI 1.23-2.18) for periodontal disease (Maisonneuve et al., Ann Oncol 2017).

risk_factors.detail: Exposure to certain chemicals and heavy metals, including beta-naphthylamine, benzidine, pesticides, asbestos, benzene, and chlorinated hydrocarbons, is associated with increased risk.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate association based on occupational studies.

risk_factors.detail: Some studies suggest an association between low plasma 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels and increased risk, but results are contradictory.
risk_factors.strength: Inconsistent evidence. Pooled analysis of case-control studies shows a positive association (OR 1.13 per 10 ng/mL increase; 95% CI 1.07-1.19) (Waterhouse et al., Ann Oncol 2015).

protective_factors: High levels of regular physical activity (associated with a 7% reduced risk compared to low activity, RR 0.93, 95% CI 0.88-0.98).
Metformin use in diabetic patients (may reduce risk, though evidence is mixed and confounded by the underlying diabetes).
Potential protective effects of certain dietary components (e.g., fruits, vegetables) though data are not consistent.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma often presents late with non-specific symptoms, leading to delayed diagnosis. The classic presentation includes painless jaundice for tumors in the pancreatic head, weight loss, and new-onset diabetes. Symptoms often develop insidiously and are nonspecific, contributing to late-stage diagnosis [MS-5].

symptoms.name: Jaundice
symptoms.detail: Painless obstructive jaundice is a hallmark for pancreatic head tumors, caused by bile duct obstruction. It is often the presenting symptom leading to diagnosis.

symptoms.name: Abdominal pain
symptoms.detail: Dull, epigastric pain that may radiate to the back, often worse after meals and when lying down. Caused by tumor invasion of retroperitoneal nerves or obstruction of the pancreatic duct.

symptoms.name: Unintentional weight loss
symptoms.detail: Significant and unintentional weight loss is a frequent symptom, often due to malabsorption, anorexia, and catabolic state.

symptoms.name: Anorexia
symptoms.detail: Loss of appetite is common and may be related to tumor effects, pain, or treatment.

symptoms.name: Nausea and vomiting
symptoms.detail: May be caused by gastric outlet obstruction from a pancreatic head tumor, or as a side effect of treatments.
symptoms.frequency: Less common, can be a late symptom

symptoms.name: Pancreatitis
symptoms.detail: New-onset pancreatitis can be the first manifestation of a pancreatic tumor obstructing the pancreatic duct.

symptoms.name: Pruritus
symptoms.detail: Generalized itching due to bile salt deposition in the skin from obstructive jaundice.

signs.name: Jaundice
signs.detail: Yellow discoloration of skin and sclera.

signs.name: Palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign)
signs.detail: A non-tender, distended gallbladder palpable in the right upper quadrant, indicative of distal bile duct obstruction.
signs.frequency: Variable, more common in pancreatic head tumors

signs.name: Ascites
signs.detail: Fluid accumulation in the abdomen, indicating peritoneal dissemination or portal hypertension.
signs.frequency: Uncommon at presentation, more common in advanced disease

signs.name: Cachexia
signs.detail: Severe weight loss and muscle wasting.

atypical_or_missed: New-onset diabetes in an older adult without classic risk factors
Deep vein thrombrosis (Trousseau syndrome) as a paraneoplastic phenomenon

red_flags: Unexplained weight loss >10% body weight over 6-12 months
Unexplained abdominal or back pain persisting >2 weeks

diagnostic.rationale: Gold standard initial imaging for diagnosis and staging. Provides detailed assessment of tumor size, location, and relationship to vasculature.
diagnostic.key_findings: Hypoencreatic mass, pancreatic duct dilation, vascular involvement, metastases. High-quality imaging can define resectability.

diagnostic.rationale: Preferred method for tissue diagnosis. Allows for high-yield biopsy and assessment of local lymph nodes.
diagnostic.key_findings: Cytological confirmation of adenocarcinoma. Sensitivity and specificity >90% for solid lesions.

diagnostic.rationale: Alternative to CT for staging, especially for evaluating indeterminate liver lesions or in patients with contrast allergy.
diagnostic.key_findings: T2-weighted hyperintense mass, biliary/pancreatic duct dilation. Excellent for characterizing cystic lesions.

diagnostic.rationale: Therapeutic for biliary decompression in jaundiced patients, not primarily diagnostic.
diagnostic.key_findings: Biliary stricture, ductal cutoff. Stent placement relieves obstruction.

diagnostic.rationale: Alternative when EUS is not feasible or for metastatic lesions (e.g., liver).

staging.rationale: Primary tool for assessing local tumor extent and resectability.
staging.indication: All patients with suspected pancreatic cancer.

staging.rationale: Complete staging to detect distant metastases (lungs, liver, peritoneum).
May detect occult metastases not seen on CT, especially in high-risk features (elevated CA 19-9, equivocal findings).
staging.indication: Consider in patients with high-risk features for metastases, or for restaging after neoadjuvant therapy.

staging.rationale: Detects small peritoneal or liver metastases missed on imaging.
staging.indication: Consider for patients with high-risk features (e.g., high CA 19-9, body/tail tumors) before laparotomy.

staging.rationale: Complementary to CT for assessing vascular involvement and lymph nodes, but not a routine staging tool.
staging.indication: If CT is equivocal for vascular invasion or for tissue diagnosis.

biomarkers.rationale: Most validated serum biomarker for diagnosis, prognosis, and monitoring response to therapy.
biomarkers.when: Baseline after biliary decompression. Used for staging, prognosis, and surveillance of recurrence.
biomarkers.mandatory: Recommended but not mandatory for all patients. Use with caution (may be elevated in benign biliary obstruction).

biomarkers.rationale: May be considered in patients who are non-secreting for CA 19-9 (Lewis antigen negative).
biomarkers.when: If CA 19-9 is not elevated despite high clinical suspicion.

biomarkers.test: Genetic testing for inherited mutations (e.g., BRCA1/2, PALB2)
biomarkers.rationale: Identifies hereditary cancer syndromes, impacts treatment (e.g., platinum sensitivity), and enables family counseling.
biomarkers.when: Recommended for all patients with confirmed pancreatic cancer.
biomarkers.mandatory: Yes, for all patients (using comprehensive gene panels).

biomarkers.rationale: Identifies actionable alterations (e.g., NTRK, BRAF, MSI, HER2, KRAS G12C) for targeted therapy.
biomarkers.when: Recommended for all patients candidates for anti-cancer therapy.

biomarkers.rationale: Potential predictive biomarker for gemcitabine benefit in adjuvant setting.

do_not_miss: Pancreatic protocol CT (high-quality, dedicated imaging is critical for staging and treatment planning)
Tissue diagnosis (via EUS-FNA preferred) before neoadjuvant or palliative therapy
Genetic testing for inherited mutations and somatic molecular profiling
CA 19-9 after adequate biliary drainage for accurate baseline
avoid: Non-contrast CT as initial imaging (poor accuracy for staging)
Routine diagnostic laparoscopy for all patients (selective based on risk)
Biopsy before surgical resection if clinical suspicion is high and resection is planned (non-diagnostic biopsy should not delay surgery)
CA 19-9 measurement in setting of active cholangitis or biliary obstruction without prior drainage (can be falsely elevated)

t_stages.description: Carcinoma in situ (includes high-grade PanIN-3, IPMN with high-grade dysplasia, MCN with high-grade dysplasia)
Tumor >0.5 cm and <1 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor >2 cm and ≤4 cm in greatest dimension
Tumor involves the celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and/or common hepatic artery, regardless of size

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed

m_stages.description: Distant metastasis (including non-regional lymph node metastasis)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Carcinoma in situ, excellent prognosis if resected.
Small, localized tumor without nodal involvement. Potentially curable with surgery.
Tumor of any size with 1-3 positive lymph nodes.

stage_groups.criteria: T1-3 N2 M0 OR T4 Any N M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Either ≥4 positive lymph nodes OR tumor involving major arteries (T4), without distant metastases. Often considered locally advanced/unresectable.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Primarily palliative (chemotherapy ± chemoradiation). Surgery considered only in selected cases after neoadjuvant therapy.

staging_pearls: T4 definition is based on arterial involvement (celiac axis, SMA, CHA), not size or venous involvement. This often defines locally advanced disease.
N2 disease (≥4 nodes) is a poor prognostic factor and often indicates need for aggressive systemic therapy.
Positive peritoneal cytology is considered M1 disease.

Resectability status (resectable, borderline resectable, locally advanced) is determined by CT and is crucial for treatment planning, often superseding AJCC stage in clinical decision-making [PANC-C].
After neoadjuvant therapy, restaging CT may not accurately reflect pathologic response; surgery should still be considered if no progression.
Adequate lymph node assessment (≥12 nodes) is recommended for accurate staging [PANC-F].

management_principles.overview: Decisions about diagnosis, resectability, and management of pancreatic cancer should involve multidisciplinary consultation at high-volume centers using appropriate imaging studies [INTRO]. The Panel unanimously endorses participation in clinical trials over standard or accepted therapy [INTRO]. Systemic therapy is used in all stages of pancreatic cancer, including neoadjuvant therapy (resectable or borderline resectable), adjuvant therapy, and first-line or subsequent therapy for locally advanced, metastatic, and recurrent disease [PANC-G]. Goals of systemic therapy should be discussed with patients prior to initiation of therapy, and enrollment in a clinical trial is strongly encouraged [PANC-G]. Less common variants, such as squamous/adenosquamous carcinomas, are treated similarly to adenocarcinoma. There are insufficient data supporting the efficacy of any of the recommended regimens for these rare subtypes. For mixed histologies, the Panel recommends treating the more aggressive histology [PANC-G, PANC-9A footnote nn]. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for any recommended systemic biologic therapy in the NCCN Guidelines [PANC-G]. Resections should be done at institutions that perform at least 15–20 pancreatic resections annually [PANC-A 1 of 8].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with resectable disease without metastases
by_intent.approach: Surgery in absence of high-risk features (without neoadjuvant therapy) or neoadjuvant therapy with or without high-risk features followed by surgery [PANC-2]. For resectable disease, upfront surgery remains an option for patients without high-risk features, though neoadjuvant therapy may also be considered [MS-12].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with borderline resectable disease and no metastatic disease
by_intent.approach: Neoadjuvant therapy followed by restaging and resection in patients without disease progression that precludes surgery. Upfront resection in patients with borderline resectable disease is no longer recommended [MS-13]. Neoadjuvant therapy is preferred at or coordinated through a high-volume center [PANC-2A footnote n].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with locally advanced disease with good or intermediate PS
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy or induction chemotherapy (preferably 4–6 months) followed by chemoradiation or SBRT in selected patients without systemic metastases. Chemoradiation or SBRT alone in patients who are not candidates for induction chemotherapy [PANC-10].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with metastatic disease and good or intermediate PS
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy, potentially with maintenance therapy after response/stable disease, or metastasis-directed therapy at a high-volume center for selected oligometastatic patients [PANC-11]. For poor PS (ECOG 3), palliative and best supportive care with consideration of single-agent chemotherapy or palliative RT [PANC-G 6 of 13].

Clinical trial preferred, or systemic therapy ± chemoradiation or SBRT if not previously done. For local recurrence in select patients, surgical re-resection may be considered after detailed restaging assessment and formulation of a multimodal therapy care plan [PANC-6, PANC-6A footnote ii].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary review should consider involving expertise from diagnostic imaging, interventional endoscopy, medical oncology, radiation oncology, surgery, pathology, geriatric medicine, genetic counseling, and palliative care. Consider consultation with a registered dietitian [PANC-1A footnote b, PANC-6 footnote b]. All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated [PANC-1].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Good PS is defined as ECOG 0–1 with good biliary drainage and adequate nutritional intake, intermediate PS is defined as ECOG 2, and poor PS is defined as ECOG 3. Patients with ECOG 4 should be exclusively supported by palliative and best supportive care [PANC-10A footnote oo, PANC-11 footnote oo, PANC-12 footnote oo, PANC-G 3 of 13 footnote e].

nodes.criteria: No arterial tumor contact (celiac axis, SMA, or CHA); no tumor contact with SMV/PV or ≤180° contact without contour irregularity [PANC-C]

options.label: Surgery in absence of high-risk features (without neoadjuvant therapy)
options.recommendation: If patient lacks high-risk features (equivocal/indeterminate imaging, markedly elevated CA 19-9, large primary tumors, large regional lymph nodes, excessive weight loss, extreme pain) [PANC-2A footnote m]

options.label: Neoadjuvant therapy with or without high-risk features followed by surgery
options.recommendation: Preferred neoadjuvant regimens include Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine, FOLFIRINOX or modified FOLFIRINOX [PANC-G 1 of 13]

adjuvant_logic.summary: For patients who did not receive prior neoadjuvant therapy: Capecitabine/Gemcitabine (category 1), modified FOLFIRINOX (category 1) are preferred [PANC-G 2 of 13]. For patients who received prior neoadjuvant therapy, adjuvant options depend on response to neoadjuvant therapy and other clinical considerations. Total duration of systemic therapy is typically 6 months [PANC-5A footnote dd].

nodes.criteria: Medically inoperable patient with resectable disease by imaging

options.label: Treat as locally advanced disease
options.recommendation: Refer to locally advanced disease pathway [PANC-2]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for medically inoperable patients.

nodes.criteria: Biopsy positive for adenocarcinoma, borderline resectable by imaging criteria [PANC-C]. Pancreatic head/uncinate: solid tumor contact with CHA without extension to CA or hepatic artery bifurcation; solid tumor contact with SMA ≤180°. Pancreatic body/tail: solid tumor contact with CA ≤180°. Venous: solid tumor contact with SMV or PV >180°, or ≤180° with contour irregularity or thrombosis [PANC-C]

options.label: Neoadjuvant therapy
options.recommendation: Upfront resection is no longer recommended. Neoadjuvant therapy preferred at or coordinated through high-volume center [PANC-3, PANC-2A footnote n]

adjuvant_logic.summary: After neoadjuvant therapy and successful resection, adjuvant therapy may be considered based on response to neoadjuvant therapy and clinical factors [PANC-5A footnote dd].

nodes.criteria: Biopsy negative or cancer not confirmed

options.label: Repeat biopsy (EUS-guided preferred)
options.recommendation: EUS-guided biopsy preferred. If cancer not confirmed after repeat biopsy, consider referral to high-volume center for evaluation [PANC-3]. If biopsy remains negative after 2-3 attempts, refer to high-volume center [MS-10].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable until diagnosis is confirmed.

nodes.criteria: Significant response: significant reduction of tumor size/volume on CT/MRI (RECIST), significant reduction of CA19-9/CEA, and/or significant reduction of PET SUV/metabolic tumor volume [PANC-D Table 1]

options.label: Surgical resection recommended
options.recommendation: Independent of degree of vascular involvement if R0 resection is technically feasible. If response warrants resection but technically unfeasible locally, second opinion at center with expertise in vascular resections recommended [PANC-D Table 1]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Consolidative chemoradiation may be considered after chemotherapy and prior to surgical resection [PANC-D Table 1].

nodes.criteria: Minimal response: stable or minimal reduction of tumor size/volume, CA19-9, and/or PET SUV [PANC-D Table 1]

options.label: Surgical resection considered in select cases
options.recommendation: In tumors without or with limited vascular involvement (without significant increased surgical risk). Otherwise, switch to different neoadjuvant therapy with response re-evaluation [PANC-D Table 1]

options.label: Switch to different neoadjuvant therapy
options.recommendation: Consider switching regimen with response re-evaluation after second-line chemotherapy [PANC-D Table 1, PANC-4]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Decision deferred pending response to alternate neoadjuvant therapy.

nodes.criteria: Disease progression not precluding surgery: increase of tumor size/volume or degree of vascular involvement on imaging, increase of CA19-9/CEA, and/or increase of PET SUV, without metastatic progression [PANC-D Table 1, PANC-4 footnote y]

options.label: Consider alternate neoadjuvant chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Switch to different neoadjuvant therapy with response re-evaluation [PANC-4, PANC-D Table 1]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Decision deferred pending response to alternate therapy.

nodes.criteria: Disease progression precluding surgery: progression with metastatic disease or local progression precluding resection [PANC-4]

options.label: Treat as locally advanced or metastatic disease
options.recommendation: Reclassify as locally advanced disease [PANC-9] or metastatic disease [PANC-11] depending on extent of disease

management_pathways.title: Postoperative Adjuvant Treatment (No Prior Neoadjuvant Therapy)

nodes.criteria: No prior neoadjuvant therapy, no evidence of recurrence or metastatic disease after resection [PANC-5]

options.label: Additional chemotherapy ± chemoradiation
options.recommendation: Adjuvant treatment should ideally be initiated within 12 weeks. Clinical trial preferred, or chemotherapy alone, or chemotherapy followed by chemoradiation as clinically indicated [PANC-5, PANC-5A footnote z]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Preferred: Capecitabine/Gemcitabine (category 1), modified FOLFIRINOX (category 1). Other recommended: Gemcitabine (category 1), Bolus Fluorouracil/Leucovorin (category 1), Continuous infusion Fluorouracil [PANC-G 2 of 13]. If considering chemoradiation, neoadjuvant or adjuvant chemotherapy should be given prior to chemoradiation [PANC-5A footnote bb]. RTOG 0848 showed survival benefit with postoperative RT in node-negative disease receiving single-agent systemic therapy [PANC-5A footnote cc].

nodes.criteria: Prior neoadjuvant therapy, no evidence of recurrence or metastatic disease [PANC-5]

options.label: Consider additional chemotherapy and/or chemoradiation as clinically indicated
options.recommendation: Adjuvant therapy options are dependent on response to neoadjuvant therapy and other clinical considerations. Total duration of systemic therapy is typically 6 months [PANC-5A footnote dd]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Patients who have received neoadjuvant chemoradiation or chemotherapy may be candidates for additional chemotherapy (or chemoradiation if none was delivered neoadjuvantly) following surgery and multidisciplinary review [PANC-5A footnote dd]. Restaging with imaging should be done after each treatment modality if chemotherapy precedes chemoradiation [PANC-5A footnote z].

nodes.criteria: Local recurrence isolated to pancreatic bed, pancreatic remnant, or mesenteric root [PANC-6, PANC-E 2 of 3]

options.label: Surgical consultation and multidisciplinary review for select patients
options.recommendation: Potential benefit of re-resection in selected patients. Carefully evaluate in multidisciplinary clinic with detailed restaging assessment and multimodal therapy care plan including neoadjuvant chemotherapy, RT, and possible surgical resection [PANC-6A footnote ii, PANC-E 2 of 3]

options.label: Systemic therapy ± chemoradiation or SBRT
options.recommendation: Clinical trial preferred or systemic therapy ± chemoradiation or SBRT if not previously done [PANC-6]

management_pathways.title: Metastatic Recurrent Disease Following Surgery
branching_basis: time from completion of primary therapy

nodes.criteria: ≥6 months from completion of primary therapy [PANC-7, PANC-G 9 of 13 footnote q]

options.label: Repeat previously administered systemic therapy or alternate systemic therapy
options.recommendation: May repeat systemic therapy previously administered or use alternate systemic therapy not previously used [PANC-7, PANC-G 9 of 13 footnote q]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for recurrent metastatic disease.

nodes.criteria: <6 months from completion of primary therapy [PANC-7, PANC-G 9 of 13 footnote q]

options.label: Switch to different drug class or alternate systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Switch to gemcitabine-based therapy if fluoropyrimidine-based therapy previously used, or vice versa [PANC-7]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for recurrent metastatic disease.

nodes.criteria: Indolent/oligometastatic disease in rare circumstances [PANC-7 footnote jj, PANC-12 footnote jj]

options.label: Refer to high-volume center for possible metastasis-directed therapy
options.recommendation: Should be referred to a high-volume center for evaluation for possible metastasis-directed therapy [PANC-12 footnote jj, reference Ludmir EB, et al. J Clin Oncol 2024;42(3 Suppl):Abstract 603]

nodes.criteria: Good or intermediate PS (ECOG 0–2), locally advanced disease (solid tumor contact >180° with SMA or CA, or not currently amenable to resection due to complete SMV/PV occlusion) [PANC-C, PANC-10]

options.label: Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Clinical trial preferred, or systemic therapy or induction chemotherapy (preferably 4–6 months) followed by chemoradiation or SBRT in selected patients without systemic metastases [PANC-10]

options.label: Chemoradiation or SBRT
options.recommendation: In patients who are not candidates for induction chemotherapy, or if poorly controlled pain or local obstructive symptoms present [PANC-10, PANC-G 3 of 13 footnote j]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for locally advanced disease.

nodes.criteria: Poor PS (ECOG 3) [PANC-10, PANC-G 4 of 13]

options.label: Palliative and best supportive care
options.recommendation: Consider single-agent chemotherapy or palliative RT [PANC-10]

options.label: Single-agent chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 over 30 minutes, weekly for 3 weeks every 28 days (category 1); Capecitabine (category 2B); Continuous infusion Fluorouracil (category 2B) [PANC-G 4 of 13]

nodes.criteria: Disease progression after initial therapy, good or intermediate PS maintained [PANC-10]

options.label: Clinical trial preferred or systemic therapy
options.recommendation: See PANC-G 8 of 13 or PANC-G 9 of 13 for subsequent therapy options [PANC-10]

options.label: Chemoradiation or SBRT
options.recommendation: If not previously given and if primary site is the sole site of progression [PANC-10]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for locally advanced disease.

management_pathways.title: Metastatic Disease — First-Line Therapy
branching_basis: molecular profiling (BRAF, NTRK, MSI-H, RET, KRAS G12C, HER2, NRG1, FGFR)

nodes.criteria: Good PS (ECOG 0–1), metastatic disease, no known actionable mutations [PANC-11, PANC-G 5 of 13]

options.label: Preferred systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine (category 1), FOLFIRINOX (category 1), NALIRIFOX (category 1), Modified FOLFIRINOX [PANC-G 5 of 13]

options.label: Other recommended regimens
options.recommendation: Erlotinib/Gemcitabine (category 1), Gemcitabine (category 1), Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Cisplatin/Gemcitabine, Capecitabine/Gemcitabine, CAPEOX (category 2B), GTX (category 2B), OFF (category 2B) [PANC-G 5 of 13]

nodes.criteria: Good PS, known BRCA1/2 or PALB2 mutation [PANC-G 5 of 13]

options.label: Platinum-based therapy preferred
options.recommendation: FOLFIRINOX (category 1), Cisplatin/Gemcitabine, Modified FOLFIRINOX [PANC-G 5 of 13]

nodes.criteria: Intermediate PS (ECOG 2), metastatic disease [PANC-G 6 of 13]

options.label: Preferred regimens
options.recommendation: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine (category 1), Capecitabine, CAPEOX, FOLFIRI, FOLFOX, Gemcitabine [PANC-G 6 of 13]

nodes.criteria: Poor PS (ECOG 3), metastatic disease [PANC-G 6 of 13]

options.label: Palliative and best supportive care
options.recommendation: Evidence suggests patients with poor PS derive marginal benefit from systemic therapy [PANC-G 6 of 13, PANC-I]

options.label: Single-agent chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 over 30 minutes weekly for 3 weeks every 28 days (category 1); Capecitabine (category 2B); Continuous infusion Fluorouracil (category 2B) [PANC-G 6 of 13]

nodes.criteria: BRAF V600E mutation-positive, any PS [PANC-G 5 of 13, PANC-G 6 of 13]

options.label: Dabrafenib/Trametinib
options.recommendation: Category 2A for good/intermediate PS; category 2B for poor PS [PANC-G 5 of 13, PANC-G 6 of 13, references 21,22]

nodes.criteria: NTRK gene fusion-positive [PANC-G 5 of 13, PANC-G 6 of 13]

options.label: Entrectinib, Larotrectinib, or Repotrectinib
options.recommendation: Repotrectinib category 2B for poor PS [PANC-G 5 of 13, PANC-G 6 of 13, reference 23]

nodes.criteria: MSI-H, dMMR, or TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb) [PANC-G 5 of 13, PANC-G 6 of 13]

options.label: Pembrolizumab
options.recommendation: Category 2B for poor PS [PANC-G 5 of 13, PANC-G 6 of 13, reference 24]

nodes.criteria: RET gene fusion-positive [PANC-G 5 of 13, PANC-G 6 of 13]

options.label: Selpercatinib
options.recommendation: Category 2B for poor PS [PANC-G 5 of 13, PANC-G 6 of 13, reference 39]

nodes.criteria: No disease progression after at least 4–6 months of chemotherapy, assuming acceptable tolerance [PANC-11, PANC-G 7 of 13]

options.label: Maintenance therapy options
options.recommendation: After platinum-based therapy with germline BRCA1/2 mutation: Olaparib [PANC-G 7 of 13, reference 435]

options.label: Other maintenance options
options.recommendation: Clinical trial; Capecitabine (after FOLFIRINOX); Modified Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine or Gemcitabine (after nab-paclitaxel/gemcitabine) [PANC-G 7 of 13]

options.label: ± Metastasis-directed therapy
options.recommendation: At a high-volume center for selected patients with indolent oligometastatic disease [PANC-11, PANC-12 footnote jj]

nodes.criteria: Good PS (ECOG 0–1), prior gemcitabine-based therapy [PANC-G 8 of 13]

options.label: Preferred subsequent therapy
options.recommendation: Fluorouracil/Leucovorin/Liposomal Irinotecan (category 1 for metastatic disease), Capecitabine, CAPEOX, Continuous infusion Fluorouracil, Bolus Fluorouracil/Leucovorin, FOLFIRI, FOLFIRINOX or modified FOLFIRINOX, FOLFOX, OFF [PANC-G 8 of 13]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for metastatic subsequent therapy.

nodes.criteria: Good PS, prior fluoropyrimidine-based therapy [PANC-G 8 of 13]

options.label: Useful in certain circumstances
options.recommendation: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine, Cisplatin/Gemcitabine (only for known BRCA1/2 or PALB2 mutations), Erlotinib/Gemcitabine, Gemcitabine, Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Cisplatin/Gemcitabine (category 2B) [PANC-G 8 of 13]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for metastatic subsequent therapy.

nodes.criteria: Intermediate PS (ECOG 2), prior fluoropyrimidine-based therapy [PANC-G 9 of 13]

options.label: Preferred subsequent therapy
options.recommendation: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine, Fluorouracil/Leucovorin/Liposomal Irinotecan (if no prior Irinotecan) [PANC-G 9 of 13]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for metastatic subsequent therapy.

nodes.criteria: Intermediate PS, prior gemcitabine-based therapy [PANC-G 9 of 13]

options.label: Preferred subsequent therapy
options.recommendation: Fluorouracil/Leucovorin/Liposomal Irinotecan (category 1 for metastatic disease), FOLFOX [PANC-G 9 of 13]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable for metastatic subsequent therapy.

nodes.criteria: Poor PS (ECOG 3), prior fluoropyrimidine-based therapy [PANC-G 10 of 13]

options.label: Palliative and best supportive care
options.recommendation: Consider single-agent chemotherapy or palliative RT [PANC-G 10 of 13]

options.label: Gemcitabine
options.recommendation: Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 over 30 minutes, weekly for 3 weeks every 28 days (category 1); Gemcitabine fixed-dose-rate may be substituted (category 2B) [PANC-G 10 of 13]

nodes.criteria: KRAS G12C mutation-positive, any PS [PANC-G 8 of 13, PANC-G 9 of 13, PANC-G 10 of 13]

options.label: Adagrasib or Sotorasib
options.recommendation: Category 2B for poor PS [PANC-G 8 of 13, PANC-G 9 of 13, PANC-G 10 of 13, references 413,414,415]

nodes.criteria: HER2-positive (IHC3+ or IHC2+ with FISH HER2 amplified), good PS [PANC-G 8 of 13]

options.label: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki
options.recommendation: Subsequent therapy only [PANC-G 8 of 13, reference 422]

nodes.criteria: NRG1 gene fusion-positive [PANC-G 8 of 13, PANC-G 9 of 13]

options.label: Zenocutuzumab-zbco
options.recommendation: For disease progression on or after prior systemic therapy, PS 0–2 [PANC-G 8 of 13, PANC-G 9 of 13, reference 423]

nodes.criteria: MSI-H or dMMR, no prior immunotherapy [PANC-G 8 of 13, PANC-G 9 of 13, PANC-G 10 of 13]

options.label: Dostarlimab-gxly or Pembrolizumab
options.recommendation: Pembrolizumab also for TMB-H ≥10 mut/Mb [PANC-G 8 of 13, PANC-G 9 of 13, PANC-G 10 of 13, references 24,431]

nodes.criteria: TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb), no prior immunotherapy, good or intermediate PS [PANC-G 8 of 13, PANC-G 9 of 13]

options.label: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
options.recommendation: Category 2B [PANC-G 8 of 13, PANC-G 9 of 13, reference 432]

items.text: Pancreatic protocol CT or MRI of abdomen (preferred CT; MRI acceptable as alternative) [PANC-1, PANC-A 1 of 8]
items.recommendation: Category 2A. High-quality dedicated imaging of the pancreas should be performed at presentation, preferably within 4 weeks of surgery, and following neoadjuvant treatment [PANC-A 1 of 8]. Multidetector CT angiography with thin sub-millimeter axial sections using dual-phase pancreatic protocol is preferred [PANC-A 1 of 8].

items.text: Chest and pelvis CT with contrast [PANC-1, PANC-A 1 of 8]
items.recommendation: Category 2A. Imaging with contrast as appropriate for disease management unless contraindicated [PANC-1A footnote c].

Category 2A. EUS to confirm primary site of involvement; EUS-guided biopsy if clinically indicated [PANC-1A footnote d]. Not recommended as a routine staging tool but may be complementary to CT [PANC-A 2 of 8].

items.text: Consider MRI as clinically indicated for indeterminate liver lesions [PANC-1]
Consider PET/CT or PET/MRI in patients with high-risk features [PANC-1, PANC-A 2 of 8]
items.recommendation: Category 2A. May be considered after formal pancreatic CT protocol to detect extra-pancreatic metastases. Not a substitute for high-quality contrast-enhanced CT [PANC-1A footnote e, PANC-A 2 of 8]. For neoadjuvant therapy, consider PET/CT or PET/MRI before and after initiation [PANC-A 2 of 8].

items.text: Consider ERCP with stent placement if jaundice present [PANC-1]
Consider staging laparoscopy as clinically indicated [PANC-2, PANC-3, PANC-9 footnote w]
items.recommendation: Category 2A. Especially in select cases with high risk for metastatic disease [PANC-2A footnote r, PANC-4A footnote w].

items.text: CA 19-9 after adequate biliary drainage [PANC-1, PANC-A 2 of 8]
items.recommendation: Category 2A. Elevated CA 19-9 does not necessarily indicate cancer or advanced disease. May be elevated due to biliary obstruction, infection, or inflammation. Undetectable in Lewis antigen-negative individuals [PANC-1A footnote g]. Consider obtaining CEA and CA-125 in patients who are nonsecreting or those with normal CA 19-9 levels [PANC-1A footnote g].

Category 2A. Core biopsy recommended, if possible, to obtain adequate tissue for molecular profiling or other ancillary studies [PANC-1A footnote i]. Biopsy proof of malignancy is not required before surgical resection when clinical suspicion is high and a non-diagnostic biopsy should not delay surgical resection [MS-10].

items.text: EUS-guided biopsy preferred for tissue acquisition [PANC-B]
items.recommendation: Category 2A. Preferably a latest generation ('core') EUS needle should be used [PANC-B]. EUS-guided biopsy is preferable to CT-guided biopsy in patients with non-metastatic disease [PANC-A 2 of 8].

items.text: Tumor/somatic molecular profiling using NGS assay [PANC-1, PANC-5, PANC-6, PANC-7, PANC-8, PANC-9]
items.recommendation: Category 2A. Recommended for ALL patients who are candidates for anti-cancer therapy. Should identify clinically actionable alterations including fusions (ALK, NRG1, NTRK, ROS1, FGFR2, RET), mutations (BRAF, BRCA1/2, KRAS, PALB2), amplifications (HER2), MSI, dMMR, TMB via FDA-approved/validated NGS-based assay. RNA sequencing recommended in addition to DNA sequencing for better detection of gene fusions. HER2 overexpression via IHC ± FISH also recommended. Testing on tumor tissue preferred; ctDNA testing can be considered if tissue testing not feasible [PANC-1A footnote j, PANC-5A footnote j].

items.text: Genetic testing for inherited mutations [PANC-1, PANC-2, PANC-5, PANC-6, PANC-7, PANC-8, PANC-9]
items.recommendation: Category 2A. Recommended for any patient with confirmed pancreatic cancer using comprehensive gene panels for hereditary cancer syndromes. Genetic counseling recommended for patients who test positive for pathogenic mutations (ATM, BRCA1, BRCA2, CDKN2A, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PALB2, PMS2, STK11, TP53) or those with positive family history of cancer, especially pancreatic cancer, regardless of mutation status [PANC-1A footnote h, PANC-2A footnote h].

items.text: ERCP with stent placement for biliary obstruction [PANC-1, PANC-B]
items.recommendation: Category 2A. Biliary drainage is not routinely recommended prior to planned upfront surgery. Consider for: symptoms of cholangitis/fever, severe symptomatic jaundice (intense pruritus), or if surgery delayed for any reason including neoadjuvant therapy [PANC-B]. SEMS preferred over plastic stents. Fully covered SEMS should be considered if tissue diagnosis not certain [PANC-B].

items.text: Performance status evaluation (ECOG) [PANC-G 3 of 13 footnote e]
items.recommendation: Category 2A. Good PS: ECOG 0–1 with good biliary drainage and adequate nutritional intake. Intermediate PS: ECOG 2. Poor PS: ECOG 3. ECOG 4: exclusively palliative and best supportive care [PANC-G 3 of 13 footnote e].

items.text: Consider evaluational geriatric assessment for older adults [PANC-G 4 of 13, PANC-G 5 of 13, PANC-G 6 of 13]
items.recommendation: Category 2A. See NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology [PANC-G 4 of 13].

items.text: Nutritional evaluation with registered dietitian [PANC-1A footnote b]
items.recommendation: Category 2A. Consider consultation with a registered dietitian [PANC-1A footnote b, PANC-I 2 of 2 footnote g].

surgery.role: Surgical resection is the only potentially curative technique for managing pancreatic cancer. The goals of surgery include an oncologic resection of the primary tumor and regional lymph nodes. Careful intraoperative staging should rule out peritoneal, liver, and distant lymph node metastases, and resection should only be done in the absence of distant disease [PANC-E 1 of 3, MS-16]. Resections should be done at institutions that perform at least 15–20 pancreatic resections annually [PANC-A 1 of 8].
principles: Achievement of an R0 resection is the primary goal, as margin-positive specimens are associated with poor long-term survival [PANC-E 1 of 3, references 1,2].

Consider frozen section analysis of the pancreatic neck and bile duct. Avoid cautery at margins sent for frozen section. If tumor located within 5 mm of margins, consider further excision to ensure at least 5 mm of clearance [PANC-E 1 of 3].

Medial dissection of pancreatic head lesions is best achieved by complete mobilization of PV and SMV from uncinate process. Skeletalization of lateral, posterior, and anterior borders of SMA down to adventitia maximizes uncinate yield and radial margin [PANC-E 1 of 3, references 3,4].

Differentiation of tumor infiltration into vein wall from tumor-related desmoplasia is frequently impossible. Liberal use of partial or complete vein excision when tumor infiltration is suspected is supported by data [PANC-E 1 of 3].
Positive cytology from washings obtained at laparoscopy or laparotomy is equivalent to M1 disease [PANC-A 2 of 8].
Spleen preservation is not indicated in adenocarcinoma [PANC-E 2 of 3].

For pancreas neck adenocarcomas, depending on extent of involvement, extended pancreaticoduodenectomy, extended distal pancreatectomy, or total pancreatectomy may be required [PANC-E 2 of 3, references 10,11].

There is a potential benefit of re-resection for recurrences in selected patients after careful multidisciplinary evaluation with detailed restaging assessment and multimodal therapy care plan [PANC-6A footnote ii, PANC-E 2 of 3].

procedures.name: Pancreatoduodenectomy (Whipple Procedure)
procedures.approach: Open or minimally invasive (laparoscopic or robotic)
procedures.indications: Adenocarcinomas of the pancreatic head and uncinate process [PANC-E 1 of 3].
procedures.key_complications: Pancreatic fistula (19.8% for pancreaticojejunostomy vs 8.0% for pancreaticogastrostomy in one trial [PANC-E 2 of 3, reference 243]), delayed gastric emptying, bile leak, hemorrhage, infection, wound complications. Pancreaticogastrostomy may be associated with lower risk of fistula formation (RR, 0.41; 95% CI, 0.21–0.62) [PANC-E 2 of 3, reference 244].
procedures.operative_mortality: <5% mortality in experienced centers [MS-16, reference 183]. Mortality is significantly lower at high-volume centers (3.8% at high-volume vs 16.3% at very-low-volume centers [MS-23, reference 277]).

procedures.name: Distal Pancreatectomy with En Bloc Splenectomy
procedures.approach: Open or minimally invasive (laparoscopic preferred for distal pancreatectomy; meta-analysis showed decreased intraoperative blood loss, time to first oral intake, and length of hospital stay [MS-20, reference 225])
procedures.indications: Adenocarcinomas of the pancreatic body and tail [PANC-E 2 of 3].
procedures.key_complications: Pancreatic fistula, bleeding, infection, splenectomy-related complications. An R0 resection mandates en bloc organ removal beyond spleen alone in up to 40% of patients [PANC-E 2 of 3, references 7,8].
procedures.operative_mortality: Data from meta-analyses show favorable perioperative outcomes with laparoscopic approach [MS-20, reference 225].

procedures.name: Total Pancreatectomy
procedures.indications: May be required when tumor diffusely involves the pancreas, is present at multiple sites, or for pancreas neck adenocarcinomas to achieve R0 resection [MS-19, PANC-E 2 of 3].
procedures.key_complications: Pancreatic exocrine and endocrine insufficiency requiring lifelong enzyme replacement and insulin management.

procedures.name: Staging Laparoscopy
procedures.indications: Diagnostic staging to rule out metastases not detected on imaging, especially for body and tail lesions, or in patients with high-risk features [PANC-A 2 of 8]. Can be performed at time of original diagnosis and following neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
procedures.key_complications: Low complication profile. Concerns about trocar-site recurrences are not well supported by clinical data [PANC-A 2 of 8, reference 86].

procedures.name: Biliary-Enteric Bypass (Open)
procedures.indications: For patients found to have unresectable disease at surgery who present with jaundice. Provides durable palliation [PANC-8, MS-48].
procedures.key_complications: May be combined with gastrojejunostomy and celiac plexus neurolysis.
procedures.operative_mortality: Palliative procedures; mortality risk should be weighed against expected survival.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is defined by tumor-vessel relationships on high-quality imaging [PANC-C]. Decisions about resectability status should be made in consensus at multidisciplinary meetings [PANC-C]. Resectable: No arterial tumor contact (CA, SMA, CHA); no SMV/PV contact or ≤180° contact without contour irregularity. Borderline Resectable (head/uncinate): solid tumor contact with CHA without extension to CA or bifurcation; solid tumor contact with SMA ≤180°; solid tumor contact with variant arterial anatomy. Borderline Resectable (body/tail): solid tumor contact with CA ≤180°. Venous borderline: solid tumor contact with SMV/PV >180°, or ≤180° with contour irregularity or thrombosis but suitable vessel for reconstruction; solid tumor contact with IVC. Locally Advanced (head/uncinate): solid tumor contact >180° with SMA or CA. Locally Advanced (body/tail): solid tumor contact >180° with SMA or CA; contact with CA and aortic involvement. Venous locally advanced: not amenable to resection due to complete SMV/PV occlusion [PANC-C]. Solid tumor contact may be replaced with increased hazy density/stranding of peripancreatic fat (seen after neoadjuvant therapy) [PANC-C footnote b].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Biliary drainage may be considered for symptomatic jaundice, cholangitis, or when surgery delayed >1 week. SEMS preferred (short, fully covered if tissue diagnosis uncertain) [PANC-B, MS-18-19]. Biliary decompression required before neoadjuvant therapy initiation in jaundiced patients [MS-20]. Nutrition optimization and performance status assessment. Preoperative CT or MRI should be performed preferably within 4 weeks of surgery [PANC-A 1 of 8]. For patients who received neoadjuvant therapy, restaging with pancreatic protocol CT/MRI and chest/pelvis CT should be performed. Consider PET/CT or PET/MRI before and after neoadjuvant therapy initiation [PANC-2A footnote o, PANC-4A footnote o]. Surgery is ideally performed 4–8 weeks after neoadjuvant therapy; surgery can be performed >8 weeks but radiation-induced fibrosis may make surgery more difficult [MS-15].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation is used in five clinical scenarios: resectable/borderline resectable (neoadjuvant setting), resected (adjuvant), locally advanced, palliative, and recurrent disease [PANC-H 1 of 7]. Goals include sterilizing vessel margins, enhancing likelihood of margin-negative resection, providing adequate local control to prevent/delay progression, and palliating pain, bleeding, or obstructive symptoms [PANC-H 1 of 7]. Based on LAP-07 trial data, there is no clear survival benefit with addition of conventional chemoradiation following gemcitabine monotherapy; however, chemoradiation may improve local control and delay the need for resumption therapy [PANC-G 3 of 13 footnote i, reference Hammel P, et al. JAMA 2016;315:1844-1853].
principles: Patients are best cared for by a multidisciplinary team [PANC-H 1 of 7].
Prior to initiation of RT, staging is optimally determined with contrast-enhanced abdominal CT and/or MRI [PANC-H 1 of 7].
IMRT is preferred over 3D-CRT for conventional or hypofractionated RT, particularly if dose escalation is considered [PANC-H 2 of 7].
A motion management strategy should be considered, including 4D-CT, respiratory gating, breath-hold, respiratory tracking, or abdominal compression [PANC-H 2 of 7].

The role and definition of elective target volume irradiation remain unclear, but emerging data suggest value to elective targeting of at-risk anatomic sites (Triangle Volume) and/or regional nodal basins. More prospective data needed [PANC-H 2 of 7, reference 6].
Fiducial markers (1–5, preferably ≥3) placed under EUS may be useful for targeting [PANC-H 2 of 7].
SBRT should be delivered at experienced, high-volume center with image-guided RT technology or in clinical trial [PANC-H 4 of 7 footnote c].
SBRT should be avoided if direct invasion of bowel or stomach is observed [PANC-H 4 of 7 footnote d].

dose_frameworks.name: Neoadjuvant/Definitive Chemoradiation
dose_frameworks.indication: Resectable/borderline resectable (neoadjuvant), locally advanced disease, recurrent disease [PANC-H 3 of 7, PANC-H 4 of 7]

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant Chemoradiation
dose_frameworks.indication: Post-resection with positive margins or high-risk features [PANC-H 3 of 7]

dose_frameworks.name: SBRT (3-fraction)
dose_frameworks.indication: Locally advanced, recurrent, or select borderline resectable disease [PANC-H 4 of 7]

dose_frameworks.name: SBRT (5-fraction)
dose_frameworks.indication: Locally advanced, recurrent, or select borderline resectable disease [PANC-H 4 of 7]

dose_frameworks.name: SBRT (hypofractionated, protracted)
dose_frameworks.indication: Locally advanced disease; caution with higher doses; normal tissue constraints must be respected [PANC-H 4 of 7]

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT
dose_frameworks.schedule: Based on burden of disease, normal tissue tolerance, and expected survival
dose_frameworks.indication: Pain, bleeding, obstructive symptoms in metastatic or non-metastatic disease [PANC-H 5 of 7]

dose_frameworks.name: Single Fraction SBRT (Celiac Plexus)
dose_frameworks.indication: Celiac axis pain relief [PANC-H 5 of 7, reference Lawrence YR, et al. Lancet Oncol 2024;25:1070-1079]

approaches.name: Neoadjuvant Chemoradiation (Resectable/Borderline Resectable)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 36 Gy in 2.4 Gy fractions to 45–54 Gy in 1.8–2.0 Gy fractions (doses higher than 54 Gy may be considered in clinical trial) [PANC-H 3 of 7]
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Capecitabine with concurrent RT or Continuous infusion Fluorouracil with concurrent RT (preferred); Gemcitabine with concurrent RT (other recommended) [PANC-G 11 of 13]
approaches.indication: Neoadjuvant setting for resectable/borderline resectable disease. Consolidative chemoradiation sometimes included following neoadjuvant chemotherapy [PANC-H 3 of 7, PANC-G 1 of 13].
approaches.key_trial: PREOPANC (Versteijne E, et al. J Clin Oncol 2020;38:1763-1773); Alliance A021101 (Katz MH, et al. JAMA Surg 2016;151:e161137)
approaches.toxicities: GI toxicities including nausea, vomiting, diarrhea; hematologic toxicities. IMRT may reduce grade 3/4 GI toxicities compared to 3D-CRT [PANC-H 6 of 7, reference 502]

approaches.name: Adjuvant Chemoradiation
approaches.dose_fractionation: 45–50.4 Gy in 1.8–2.0 Gy fractions (25–28 fx) to tumor bed, surgical anastomoses, and adjacent lymph node basins [PANC-H 3 of 7]
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Capecitabine with concurrent RT or Continuous infusion Fluorouracil with concurrent RT (preferred); Gemcitabine with concurrent RT (other recommended) [PANC-G 11 of 13]
approaches.indication: Post-resection for patients with positive resection margins or other high-risk features; primarily in node-negative disease based on RTOG 0848 [PANC-5A footnote cc, PANC-H 3 of 7]
approaches.key_trial: RTOG 0848 (Abrams RA, et al. J Clin Oncol 2024;42(16 Suppl):Abstract 4005) showed survival benefit with postoperative RT in node-negative disease receiving single-agent systemic therapy
approaches.toxicities: GI toxicities, hematologic toxicities; normal tissue constraints for stomach, duodenum, liver, kidneys, spinal cord [PANC-H 5 of 7]

approaches.name: Induction Chemotherapy Followed by Chemoradiation or SBRT (Locally Advanced)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 45–56 Gy in 1.8–2.2 Gy fractions for chemoradiation; SBRT 3–5 fractions [PANC-H 4 of 7]
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Fluoropyrimidine-based concurrent chemotherapy for chemoradiation [PANC-H 4 of 7]
approaches.indication: Locally advanced disease without systemic metastases in patients with good/intermediate PS. Induction chemotherapy (preferably 4–6 months) followed by consolidation chemoradiation [PANC-10, PANC-H 4 of 7]
approaches.key_trial: LAP-07 (Hammel P, et al. JAMA 2016;315:1844-1853) showed no clear survival benefit but may improve local control; SCALOP (Mukherjee S, et al. Lancet Oncol 2013;14:317-326) favored capecitabine-based chemoradiation
approaches.toxicities: Similar to above; SBRT may result in less severe radiation-induced lymphopenia compared to conventional chemoradiation [MS-47, reference 451]

approaches.name: Upfront Chemoradiation or SBRT (Locally Advanced)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 45–56 Gy in 1.8–2.2 Gy fractions for chemoradiation; SBRT 3–5 fractions [PANC-H 4 of 7]
approaches.indication: Patients not candidates for induction chemotherapy, or presenting with poorly controlled pain or local obstructive symptoms [PANC-10, PANC-H 4 of 7, PANC-G 3 of 13 footnote j]
approaches.key_trial: ECOG-4201 (Loehrer PJ, et al. J Clin Oncol 2011;29:4105-4112) showed OS benefit for chemoradiation vs chemotherapy alone but limited by poor accrual

approaches.name: Chemoradiation or SBRT for Recurrent Disease
approaches.dose_fractionation: 45–56 Gy in 1.8–2.2 Gy fractions for chemoradiation; SBRT 3–5 fractions [PANC-H 4 of 7]
approaches.indication: Local recurrence in the pancreatic bed if not previously performed; only if primary site is sole site of progression [PANC-6, PANC-H 4 of 7]
approaches.key_trial: Limited data specific to recurrent disease setting
approaches.toxicities: GI toxicities; must respect normal tissue constraints given prior treatment [PANC-H 4 of 7]

approaches.name: Palliative Radiation Therapy
approaches.dose_fractionation: Variable; short course for painful bony metastases; single fraction SBRT for celiac axis pain [PANC-H 5 of 7]
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: With or without concurrent chemotherapy [PANC-H 5 of 7]
approaches.indication: Pain, bleeding, obstructive symptoms in metastatic or non-metastatic disease; osseous pain from bone metastases [PANC-H 5 of 7]
approaches.key_trial: Lawrence YR, et al. Lancet Oncol 2024;25:1070-1079 (celiac plexus radiosurgery for pain management)
approaches.toxicities: Should take into account burden of disease, normal tissue tolerance, and expected survival [PANC-H 5 of 7]

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is used in all stages of pancreatic cancer including neoadjuvant therapy (resectable/borderline resectable), adjuvant therapy, and first-line or subsequent therapy for locally advanced, metastatic, and recurrent disease [PANC-G]. Consider testing for actionable somatic findings including fusions (ALK, NRG1, NTRK, ROS1, FGFR2, RET), mutations (BRAF, BRCA1/2, KRAS, PALB2), amplifications (HER2), MSI, dMMR, TMB using FDA-approved/validated NGS-based assay, and HER2 overexpression via IHC ± FISH [PANC-G]. If BRCA1/2 status is unknown, treat with systemic therapy as clinically indicated [PANC-G]. For information regarding DPYD testing, see NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer [PANC-G]. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV Pembrolizumab with different dosing and administration instructions [PANC-G].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine; FOLFIRINOX or modified FOLFIRINOX (limited to ECOG 0–1); Cisplatin/Gemcitabine (only for known BRCA1/2 or PALB2 mutations, ≥2 to 6 cycles) [PANC-G 1 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Limited evidence to recommend specific neoadjuvant regimens off-study. Practices vary regarding use of chemotherapy and radiation. Consolidative chemoradiation sometimes included. Most NCCN Member Institutions prefer neoadjuvant therapy at or coordinated through high-volume center [PANC-G 1 of 13, PANC-2A footnote n]. PAXG showed improved EFS compared to mFOLFIRINOX in resectable/borderline resectable disease (median EFS 16.0 vs 10.2 months; HR 0.63; P=.0018) but listed as useful in certain circumstances due to limited institutional experience [PANC-G 1 of 13, reference 2].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: BRCA1/2 or PALB2 mutation status for Cisplatin/Gemcitabine eligibility [PANC-G 1 of 13]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Capecitabine/Gemcitabine (category 1); Modified FOLFIRINOX (category 1, limited to ECOG 0–1) [PANC-G 2 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ESPAC-4: Gemcitabine/Capecitabine superior to Gemcitabine alone (HR 0.82; 95% CI 0.68–0.98; P=.032; median OS 28.0 vs 25.5 months) [reference 5]. PRODIGE 24/CCTG PA.6: mFOLFIRINOX superior to Gemcitabine (5-year DFS 26.1% vs 19.0%; median OS 53.5 vs 35.5 months) [reference 1].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Bolus Fluorouracil/Leucovorin (category 1); Gemcitabine (category 1); Continuous infusion Fluorouracil; Capecitabine (category 2B); Chemotherapy followed by chemoradiation ± subsequent chemotherapy [PANC-G 2 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: None specifically required for standard adjuvant therapy selection [PANC-G 2 of 13]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine; FOLFIRINOX or modified FOLFIRINOX (ECOG 0–1); NALIRIFOX; Cisplatin/Gemcitabine or FOLFIRINOX/mFOLFIRINOX (only for known BRCA1/2 or PALB2 mutations) [PANC-G 3 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine and FOLFIRINOX/mFOLFIRINOX recommendations based on extrapolations from randomized trials in metastatic disease [PANC-G 3 of 13 footnote f]. NALIRIFOX: high-level evidence over Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine, but does not appear to have advantage over FOLFIRINOX [PANC-G 3 of 13 footnote g].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine/Gemcitabine; Erlotinib/Gemcitabine; Gemcitabine; Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Cisplatin/Gemcitabine (category 2B); Capecitabine (category 2B); CAPEOX (category 2B); Continuous infusion Fluorouracil (category 2B); GTX (category 2B); OFF (category 2B); Induction chemotherapy followed by chemoradiation or SBRT [PANC-G 3 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: BRCA1/2 or PALB2 mutations for platinum-based options; BRAF V600E for Dabrafenib/Trametinib; NTRK fusions for Entrectinib/Larotrectinib/Repotrectinib; MSI-H/dMMR/TMB-H for Pembrolizumab; RET fusions for Selpercatinib [PANC-G 3 of 13]

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Reduced intensity regimens appropriate for intermediate PS [PANC-G 4 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Induction chemotherapy followed by chemoradiation or SBRT; Chemoradiation or SBRT alone; Targeted/immunotherapy based on molecular profiling [PANC-G 4 of 13]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 over 30 minutes, weekly for 3 weeks every 28 days (category 1) [PANC-G 4 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Single-agent therapy for poor PS; marginal benefit from systemic therapy [PANC-G 4 of 13 footnote l]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Capecitabine (category 2B); Continuous infusion Fluorouracil (category 2B); Gemcitabine fixed-dose-rate may be substituted (category 2B) [PANC-G 4 of 13]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine (category 1); FOLFIRINOX (category 1); NALIRIFOX (category 1); Modified FOLFIRINOX [PANC-G 5 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: MPACT: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine improved OS vs Gemcitabine alone (8.5 vs 6.7 months; P<.001; HR 0.72) [reference 7]. FOLFIRINOX: OS 11.1 vs 6.8 months (P<.001) [reference 8]. NALIRIFOX: NAPOLI-3 showed significant OS and PFS improvement over Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine (reference 9).
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Erlotinib/Gemcitabine (category 1); Gemcitabine (category 1); Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Cisplatin/Gemcitabine; Capecitabine/Gemcitabine; CAPEOX (category 2B); GTX (category 2B); OFF (category 2B); Targeted therapy based on molecular profiling [PANC-G 5 of 13]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine (category 1); Capecitabine; CAPEOX; FOLFIRI; FOLFOX; Gemcitabine [PANC-G 6 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Reduced intensity options appropriate for intermediate PS [PANC-G 6 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Targeted/immunotherapy based on molecular profiling [PANC-G 6 of 13]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Palliative and best supportive care [PANC-G 6 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Evidence suggests marginal benefit from systemic therapy in poor PS [PANC-G 6 of 13 footnote l]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 over 30 minutes, weekly for 3 weeks every 28 days (category 1); Capecitabine (category 2B); Continuous infusion Fluorouracil (category 2B); Gemcitabine fixed-dose-rate (category 2B) [PANC-G 6 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Targeted/immunotherapy options available even for poor PS [PANC-G 6 of 13]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Metastatic Disease (Maintenance, Good/Intermediate PS, Prior Platinum with Germline BRCA1/2)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: POLO trial: Olaparib vs placebo PFS 7.4 vs 3.8 months (P=.004) [reference 435]. No OS difference at interim analysis [PANC-G 7 of 13].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Clinical trial; Capecitabine (after FOLFIRINOX); Fluorouracil ± Irinotecan (for oxaliplatin-related neuropathy/allergy); FOLFOX (if GI toxicity concern); Modified Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine or Gemcitabine (after nab-paclitaxel/gemcitabine) [PANC-G 7 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Germline BRCA1/2 mutation for olaparib [PANC-G 7 of 13]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Subsequent Therapy (Locally Advanced/Metastatic, Good PS 0–1, Prior Gemcitabine)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Fluorouracil/Leucovorin/Liposomal Irinotecan (category 1 for metastatic disease); Capecitabine; CAPEOX; Continuous infusion Fluorouracil; Bolus Fluorouracil/Leucovorin; FOLFIRI; FOLFIRINOX or modified FOLFIRINOX; FOLFOX; OFF [PANC-G 8 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: NAPOLI-1: Nanoliposomal irinotecan + FU/LV improved PFS (3.1 vs 1.5 months; HR 0.56; P<.001) and OS (6.2 vs 4.2 months; HR 0.75; P=.042) vs FU/LV alone [reference 32]. CONKO-003: OFF improved OS vs FU/LV (5.9 vs 3.3 months; HR 0.66; P=.01) [reference 400].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine; Cisplatin/Gemcitabine (BRCA1/2/PALB2 only); Erlotinib/Gemcitabine; Gemcitabine; Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Cisplatin/Gemcitabine (category 2B) [PANC-G 8 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: KRAS G12C for Adagrasib/Sotorasib; BRAF V600E for Dabrafenib/Trametinib; NTRK for Entrectinib/Larotrectinib/Repotrectinib; FGFR alterations for Erdafitinib; HER2 for Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki; RET for Selpercatinib; NRG1 for Zenocutuzumab-zbco; MSI-H/dMMR for Dostarlimab/Pembrolizumab; TMB-H for Ipilimumab+Nivolumab [PANC-G 8 of 13]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Palliative and best supportive care [PANC-G 10 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Marginal benefit from systemic therapy in poor PS [PANC-G 10 of 13 footnote l]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Gemcitabine (prior fluoropyrimidine); Capecitabine or Continuous infusion Fluorouracil (prior gemcitabine); Targeted therapy based on molecular profiling [PANC-G 10 of 13]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Same molecular profiling as above [PANC-G 10 of 13]

key_regimens.name: Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic first-line (category 1); Locally advanced first-line; Adjuvant (with consolidative chemoradiation); Subsequent therapy
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia (most common grade ≥3), fatigue, neuropathy [PANC-G 3 of 13, reference 7]. Development of peripheral neuropathy associated with longer treatment duration and improved survival.

key_regimens.name: FOLFIRINOX
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic first-line (category 1); Locally advanced first-line; Neoadjuvant (ECOG 0–1)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia (45.7% grade 3/4), diarrhea (12.7%), thrombocytopenia (9.1%), sensory neuropathy (9.0%) [PANC-G 3 of 13, reference 8]. Due to high toxicity, bolus fluorouracil often omitted [PANC-G 1 of 13 footnote c].

key_regimens.name: Modified FOLFIRINOX
key_regimens.setting: Adjuvant (category 1); Metastatic first-line; Locally advanced first-line; Neoadjuvant
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Significantly less neutropenia, fatigue, vomiting compared to standard FOLFIRINOX [reference 334]. In adjuvant setting (PRODIGE 24): 5-year DFS 26.1% vs 19.0% with gemcitabine; median OS 53.5 vs 35.5 months [reference 1].

key_regimens.name: NALIRIFOX (Liposomal Irinotecan/Fluorouracil/Leucovorin/Oxaliplatin)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Manageable and tolerated in phase I/II [reference 391]. NAPOLI-3 showed significant OS and PFS improvement vs Albumin-bound Paclitaxel/Gemcitabine [reference 392]. Does not appear to have advantage over FOLFIRINOX [PANC-G 3 of 13 footnote g].

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine (BRCA1/2 or PALB2 only)
key_regimens.setting: Neoadjuvant (≥2–6 cycles); Locally advanced first-line; Metastatic first-line; Subsequent therapy
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, nausea/vomiting. Only for known BRCA1/2 or PALB2 mutations [PANC-G 1 of 13, PANC-G 5 of 13, references 10,11].

key_regimens.name: Gemcitabine Monotherapy
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic first-line (category 1); Locally advanced first-line (category 2A); Adjuvant (category 1); Subsequent therapy
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, flu-like symptoms, elevated transaminases, fatigue. Adjuvant setting (CONKO-001): median OS 22.8 vs 20.2 months vs observation (HR 0.76; P=.01) [reference 378].

key_regimens.name: Gemcitabine Fixed-Dose-Rate
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic first-line (poor PS); Locally advanced (poor PS)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: May be substituted for standard infusion in patients with poor PS (category 2B) [PANC-G 4 of 13, PANC-G 6 of 13]. ECOG-6201: FDR gemcitabine median OS 6.2 vs 4.9 months for standard infusion (P=.04) [reference 343].

key_regimens.name: Fluorouracil/Leucovorin/Liposomal Irinotecan
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy for locally advanced/metastatic disease (category 1 for metastatic disease with prior gemcitabine)

drugs.name: Liposomal Irinotecan
drugs.dose: 70 mg/m2 (or 80 mg/m2 if no prior irinotecan)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia (27% grade 3/4), fatigue (14%), diarrhea (13%), vomiting (11%) [reference 32]. NAPOLI-1: PFS 3.1 vs 1.5 months (HR 0.56; P<.001); OS 6.2 vs 4.2 months (HR 0.75; P=.042) vs FU/LV [reference 32].

key_regimens.name: Erlotinib/Gemcitabine
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic first-line (category 1); Locally advanced first-line; Subsequent therapy
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Rash, diarrhea. Although combination significantly improved survival, actual benefit was small (HR 0.82; OS 6.24 vs 5.91 months) suggesting only a small subset benefits [reference 363].

key_regimens.name: Capecitabine/Gemcitabine
key_regimens.setting: Adjuvant (category 1, preferred); Metastatic first-line; Locally advanced first-line
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea. Adjuvant (ESPAC-4): OS 28.0 vs 25.5 months for gemcitabine alone (HR 0.82; 95% CI 0.68–0.98; P=.032) [reference 5].

key_regimens.name: Olparib (Maintenance, BRCA1/2 germline only)
key_regimens.setting: Maintenance therapy for metastatic disease with germline BRCA1/2 mutations and no progression after 4–6 months of platinum-based first-line therapy
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade ≥3 AEs 40% vs 23% with placebo. POLO trial: PFS 7.4 vs 3.8 months (P=.004); no OS difference at interim analysis (18.9 vs 16.1 months; P=.68) [reference 435].

key_regimens.name: FOLFOX
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy (intermediate PS); Metastatic first-line (intermediate PS); Maintenance
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neuropathy, myelosuppression, nausea. Note: PANCREOX showed possible detriment in second-line setting after gemcitabine progression [reference 396]; may be considered in intermediate PS patients who cannot tolerate FOLFIRINOX [PANC-G 6 of 13, PANC-G 9 of 13].

key_regimens.name: FOLFIRI
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic first-line (intermediate PS 2); Subsequent therapy (good PS)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, diarrhea. Phase II trials show comparable efficacy and safety [references 401,402]. Median OS 6.6 months in second-line setting [reference 402].

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab (MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H)
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent therapy (if no prior immunotherapy); First-line for MSI-H/dMMR

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.day: Day 1 every 3 weeks (or 400 mg every 6 weeks)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related AEs (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies). KEYNOTE-158: ORR 34.3%; median PFS 4.1 months; median OS 23.5 months in MSI-H/dMMR tumors [references 24,427].

key_regimens.name: Larotrectinib/Entrectinib (NTRK fusion-positive)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Generally well-tolerated. Larotrectinib: ORR 79% (95% CI 72%–85%); 16% complete response [references 406,407]. Entrectinib: ORR 75%; median DOR 12.9 months [references 408,409,410].

key_regimens.name: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (BRAF V600E)
key_regimens.setting: First-line metastatic (good/intermediate PS, category 2A; poor PS, category 2B); Subsequent therapy
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fever, fatigue, nausea. NCI-MATCH Subprotocol H and ROAR basket trial showed efficacy and acceptable safety [references 21,22].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Treatment Response Assessment in Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Serial imaging as indicated to assess disease response. For neoadjuvant therapy, restaging should include pancreatic protocol CT or MRI of abdomen, chest/pelvis CT, consider PET, post-treatment CA 19-9, and consider staging laparoscopy as clinically indicated [PANC-4, PANC-10 footnote pp]. Surgery is ideally performed 4–8 weeks after neoadjuvant therapy completion, though can be performed >8 weeks (radiation-induced fibrosis may make surgery more difficult) [MS-15]. For locally advanced/metastatic disease, serial CT with contrast (routine single portal venous phase or dedicated pancreatic protocol if surgery still contemplated) or MRI with contrast of known sites of disease to determine therapeutic benefit [PANC-A 2 of 8].
response_logic: Response can be determined by imaging (RECIST criteria) and/or other clinical parameters including change in performance status, pain, satiety, weight/nutritional status, diabetes control [PANC-D, MS-15].
For neoadjuvant therapy: four response categories defined — significant, minimal, disease progression not precluding surgery, and disease progression precluding surgery [PANC-4, PANC-D Table 1].
Disease progression is defined by rising CA 19-9 or enlargement of the mass [PANC-4A footnote y].

Retrospective studies suggest imaging characteristics may not be a reliable indicator of resectability after neoadjuvant therapy. Determinations of resectability should be made on individualized basis in multidisciplinary setting [PANC-A 2 of 8, MS-14].

Radiographic response does not correlate with pathologic response in borderline resectable disease. If no apparent tumor shrinkage after neoadjuvant treatment and no extrapancreatic progressive disease, surgery should still be attempted [MS-14, references 144,145].

In marked radiographic improvement, patient should be referred to high-volume center for consideration of surgery. If radiographic stability with marked clinical improvement or decline in CA 19-9, patient should still be referred [PANC-10 footnote ss].

Mixed responses (reduction in tumor size on CT but increase in CA 19-9): third response metric (PET response) and multidisciplinary tumor board discussion may assist in determining category (2 out of 3 metrics) [PANC-D footnote c].
Negative predictive value of CA 19-9: low positive predictive value for screening but useful for monitoring treatment response and prognosis in various settings [MS-10-11].
imaging_recommendations: Pancreatic protocol CT or MRI after neoadjuvant therapy for restaging [PANC-4, PANC-A 1 of 8].
Chest/pelvis CT with contrast for complete staging [PANC-4, PANC-A 1 of 8].
PET/CT or PET/MRI before and after neoadjuvant therapy initiation to assess metabolic response and for restaging [PANC-2A footnote o, PANC-A 2 of 8, PANC-D footnote f].
For locally advanced/metastatic disease: serial CT with contrast or MRI with contrast of known sites of disease [PANC-A 2 of 8].
