In the adjuvant setting: baseline postoperative CT (chest, abdomen, pelvis) with contrast [PANC-5].
Radiology reporting template recommended (morphologic, arterial, venous, extrapancreatic evaluation) [PANC-A 5-8 of 8].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Biopsy confirmation recommended after neoadjuvant therapy if there is a question about response or if patient is not surgical candidate, to distinguish viable tumor from treatment-related changes [PANC-A 2 of 8].
Re-biopsy recommended if adequate tissue is not available for molecular profiling in metastatic/recurrent disease [PANC-12, PANC-G 8 of 13].
Positive cytology from laparoscopy or laparotomy washings is equivalent to M1 disease [PANC-A 2 of 8].
For local recurrence after resection: consider confirmatory biopsy (category 2B), genetic testing for inherited mutations, and molecular profiling if not previously done [PANC-6].
ctDNA to assess minimal residual disease: recognized as emerging tool but no consensus recommendation due to limited data on how results would guide treatment decision-making [PANC-5A footnote z].
For patients with unresectable disease at surgery: biopsy confirmation of diagnosis if not previously done [PANC-8].

prognosis.overall: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma carries a poor prognosis, with an estimated 5-year overall survival rate of 11% for all stages combined. In 2026, an estimated 67,530 people will be diagnosed with pancreatic cancer and 52,740 will die from the disease in the United States, making it the fourth leading cause of cancer-related death. Prognosis is heavily influenced by disease stage at diagnosis, with only a minority of patients presenting with localized disease amenable to curative-intent resection. Even after complete resection, recurrence rates are high. Systemic therapy has modestly improved outcomes for advanced disease, but survival remains measured in months for most patients with metastatic disease.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Data for stage-specific 5-year survival are not explicitly provided in the source. However, patients with resectable disease (which includes most stage I) who undergo curative-intent surgery followed by adjuvant therapy have median overall survival ranging from 20.1 to 28.0 months under optimal clinical trial conditions.
by_stage.context: Includes resectable and potentially borderline resectable tumors. Treatment involves surgery with or without neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy.

by_stage.stage: Stage II (T1-3, N1, M0 or T3, N0, M0)
by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly provided. Survival is intermediate, heavily dependent on nodal status (N1 vs N2) and response to therapy.
by_stage.context: Often borderline resectable or locally advanced. Multimodality therapy including neoadjuvant/induction chemotherapy and possible chemoradiation is standard.

by_stage.stage: Stage III (Any T, N2, M0 or T4, Any N, M0)
by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly provided. Prognosis is poor for locally advanced disease, with median overall survival typically 12-18 months with modern systemic regimens.
by_stage.context: Locally advanced, unresectable disease. Primary treatment is systemic therapy with possible consolidation chemoradiation or SBRT.

by_stage.stage: Stage IV (Any T, Any N, M1)
by_stage.five_yr_survival: Less than 3%. Median overall survival with first-line chemotherapy ranges from approximately 6.8 to 12.1 months depending on regimen and performance status.
by_stage.context: Metastatic disease. Treatment is palliative systemic therapy with a goal of prolonging survival and maintaining quality of life.

prognostic_factors: Resection status (R0 vs. R1/R2) is a major prognostic factor for resected disease.
Lymph node involvement (N1/N2 status and lymph node ratio) strongly influences prognosis after resection.
Performance status (ECOG 0-1 vs. 2-3) is critical for tolerating therapy and survival in advanced disease.
CA 19-9 levels: elevated baseline levels and failure to normalize with treatment are associated with poorer outcomes.
Presence of specific molecular alterations (e.g., germline BRCA1/2 mutations, MSI-H/dMMR) may influence response to specific therapies and thus prognosis.
Disease stage at presentation (resectable, borderline resectable, locally advanced, metastatic).
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis varies dramatically based on stage at diagnosis, with only a small fraction of patients presenting with truly resectable disease. The biology of pancreatic cancer is aggressive, with a high propensity for early micrometastatic dissemination. Even with optimal multimodality treatment, recurrence rates after resection are high, and median survival for metastatic disease remains measured in months. Multidisciplinary care at high-volume centers is associated with improved surgical outcomes and may influence overall prognosis.

disease_related.management: Endoscopic placement of a self-expandable metal stent (SEMS) is preferred for palliation. Percutaneous biliary drainage or surgical biliary-enteric bypass are alternatives if endoscopic access fails or is not feasible. Biliary decompression is required before initiation of neoadjuvant therapy for jaundiced patients.

Endoscopic enteral stent placement is preferred for patients with poor performance status. Gastrojejunostomy (open or laparoscopic) may be considered for fit patients with life expectancy >3-6 months. A venting PEG tube can be used for gastric decompression in very poor prognosis patients.

Low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) is preferred over warfarin. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) may be considered for patients without luminal tumors. Prophylactic LMWH is not routinely recommended due to lack of proven survival benefit.

Early referral to pain or palliative care specialist. Opioid analgesics are mainstay. EUS-guided celiac plexus neurolysis is recommended for pain refractory to opioids. Palliative radiation therapy or SBRT may be used for local control of pain. High-intensity focused ultrasound and intrathecal drug delivery are options for refractory cases.
Therapeutic endoscopy if clinically indicated. Palliative radiation therapy if not previously administered. Angiography with embolization for clinically significant bleeding.

Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy with a starting dose of at least 48,000 units lipase with meals (preferably 72,000). Nutritional evaluation with a registered dietitian. Consider proton pump inhibitors if enzyme replacement is ineffective.

chemotherapy: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia) - G-CSF support may be needed.
Diarrhea - managed with loperamide and dietary modifications.
Peripheral neuropathy - dose modification or discontinuation of neurotoxic agents (e.g., oxaliplatin, paclitaxel).
Nausea and vomiting - antiemetic prophylaxis as per institutional guidelines.
Mucositis/stomatitis - oral care and supportive measures.
Hepatotoxicity - monitoring of liver function tests.
immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) including colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies (hypothyroidism, hypophysitis), dermatologic toxicity, and nephritis.

Management requires early recognition, corticosteroids, and possibly additional immunosuppressants (e.g., infliximab for colitis). Referral to the NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities is recommended.
targeted_therapy: Specific toxicities depend on the agent: e.g., dabrafenib/trametinib can cause fever, rash, and cardiac dysfunction; olaparib can cause nausea, fatigue, and myelosuppression.
Monitoring as per drug-specific guidelines is essential.
surgery: Pancreatic fistula - a major source of morbidity; prevention includes meticulous surgical technique and possible use of pasireotide.

supportive_care.overview: The primary goal of palliative and supportive care is to prevent and ameliorate suffering while ensuring optimal quality of life. This includes management of disease-related symptoms (e.g., biliary obstruction, pain, malnutrition) and treatment-related side effects. A multidisciplinary approach involving medical oncology, palliative care, nutrition, pain management, and psychosocial support is essential. Palliative surgical procedures are best reserved for patients with longer life expectancies.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Pancreatic exocrine enzyme replacement therapy is recommended for all patients with symptoms of exocrine insufficiency, starting with at least 48,000 units lipase with meals (preferably 72,000). A nutritional evaluation by a registered dietitian is recommended for all patients with locally advanced or metastatic disease. Nutritional support may include oral supplements, enteral feeding via jejunostomy tube, or parenteral nutrition in select cases.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Anti-emetic prophylaxis should be based on the emetogenic potential of the chemotherapy regimen used. For highly emetogenic regimens (e.g., FOLFIRINOX), a combination of a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist, dexamethasone, and an NK1 receptor antagonist is recommended. For moderately emetogenic regimens, a 5-HT3 antagonist plus dexamethasone is appropriate.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Growth factor support (G-CSF) should be considered for patients receiving chemotherapy regimens with a high risk of febrile neutropenia (>20%) or in patients with additional risk factors (e.g., advanced age, poor performance status, prior episodes of neutropenia). The use of pegfilgrastim is preferred for convenience.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Patients with pancreatic cancer are at high risk for VTE. Treatment-dose LMWH is preferred over warfarin for established VTE. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) may be considered for patients without luminal tumors. Prophylactic anticoagulation is not routinely recommended outside of a clinical trial setting due to lack of proven survival benefit.
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain management should follow the NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain. Early referral to a pain specialist is recommended. For severe tumor-associated abdominal pain refractory to opioids, EUS-guided celiac plexus neurolysis is a preferred option. Palliative radiation therapy or SBRT may be used for local control of pain. High-intensity focused ultrasound and intrathecal drug delivery are options for refractory cases.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: All patients should be screened for depression, anxiety, and distress using validated tools. Referral to palliative medicine services, social work, and psychological support is recommended. Advance care planning should be encouraged.
supportive_care.dental_care: Dental care is not specifically addressed in the guidelines. However, good oral hygiene is important for patients receiving chemotherapy, and a dental evaluation may be considered prior to initiation of therapy.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Following curative-intent surgery, patients should undergo a baseline postoperative CT (chest, abdomen, and pelvis) with contrast and measurement of CA 19-9. If not previously done, genetic testing for inherited mutations, biopsy confirmation, and molecular profiling should be performed. Adjuvant therapy should be initiated within 12 weeks of recovery from surgery. The preferred adjuvant regimen is modified FOLFIRINOX or capecitabine/gemcitabine.
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: The rationale for surveillance is the early detection of recurrence, which may allow for potential enrollment in clinical trials or other forms of treatment. However, data on the effectiveness of surveillance in improving overall survival are limited. Earlier identification of recurrence is a consensus goal.
late_effects_screening: Monitor for treatment-related toxicities, such as peripheral neuropathy (from oxaliplatin or paclitaxel), renal dysfunction (from cisplatin), and cardiotoxicity (from specific agents).
Screen for post-pancreatectomy diabetes and exocrine insufficiency, which may require ongoing enzyme replacement.
Screen for nutritional deficiencies and malnutrition.
Monitor for psychological distress, depression, and fatigue.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Recurrence after resection can be local (pancreatic bed), regional (lymph nodes), or distant (metastatic). The most common site of recurrence for patients followed for 5 years is the lungs. Patterns of recurrence can be influenced by tumor stage, margin status, and lymph node involvement.

clinical_follow_up_schedule: History and physical examination for symptom assessment at each visit.
Frequency is typically every 3-6 months for the first 2-3 years, then every 6-12 months thereafter, though specific intervals are not mandated in the source.
imaging_strategy: Chest CT and CT or MRI of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast (unless contraindicated) as clinically indicated.
Typically performed every 3-6 months for the first 2-3 years, then annually if no evidence of recurrence.
Imaging should be repeated prior to adjuvant therapy to assess for early recurrence.
laboratory_monitoring: CA 19-9 level measurement is a category 2B recommendation. It should be measured after neoadjuvant treatment, prior to surgery, following surgery immediately prior to adjuvant therapy, and for surveillance.
CA 19-9 elevation alone, without other evidence of recurrence, is not a clear indication for treatment.
Liver function tests should be monitored periodically.
supportive_follow_up: Nutritional assessment and support as needed.
Monitoring for and management of exocrine pancreatic insufficiency with enzyme replacement therapy.

special_situations.key_message: Neoadjuvant therapy is strongly preferred over upfront surgery to improve the likelihood of an R0 resection and to select patients with stable or responsive disease for surgery.
special_situations.management: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (e.g., FOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel) with or without consolidative chemoradiation. Restaging with pancreatic protocol CT/MRI, CA 19-9, and consider staging laparoscopy after neoadjuvant therapy. Surgical resection is considered for patients without disease progression who are deemed resectable upon restaging.
special_situations.evidence: Based on retrospective data and single-arm phase II trials showing feasibility, R0 resection rates of 31-68%, and median OS of 21.7 months in select cohorts (reference 149). No phase III trials comparing neoadjuvant vs upfront surgery in this setting have been completed.

special_situations.key_message: The primary goal is palliation and survival prolongation. Treatment is based on performance status, with induction systemic therapy followed by possible consolidation local therapy for selected patients.
special_situations.management: For good PS (ECOG 0-1): systemic therapy (e.g., FOLFIRINOX, gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, NALIRIFOX) followed by chemoradiation or SBRT in select patients who do not develop metastatic disease. For intermediate PS (ECOG 2): systemic therapy. For poor PS (ECOG 3): palliative and best supportive care, with consideration of single-agent chemotherapy or palliative RT.
special_situations.evidence: Based on extrapolation from metastatic disease trials and phase II/III trials in locally advanced disease (e.g., LAP-07 trial showed no OS benefit for chemoradiation after gemcitabine, but may improve local control, reference 15).

special_situations.key_message: Treatment is palliative, aiming to prolong survival and maintain quality of life. Systemic therapy is recommended for good/intermediate PS. Maintenance therapy options exist for patients with stable disease after initial chemotherapy.
special_situations.management: First-line: systemic therapy based on PS and molecular profile (e.g., FOLFIRINOX, gemcitabine/nab-paclitaxel, NALIRIFOX). Maintenance therapy for patients with no progression after 4-6 months may include olaparib for germline BRCA1/2 mutations, capecitabine after FOLFIRINOX, or a holiday from chemotherapy. Subsequent therapy is based on prior treatment and molecular profiling.
special_situations.evidence: Category 1 evidence for first-line regimens from phase III trials (e.g., PRODIGE 4/ACCORD 11, MPACT, NAPOLI-3). POLO trial established olaparib maintenance for germline BRCA-mutated disease.

special_situations.key_message: Management depends on the pattern of recurrence (local vs. metastatic) and time from prior therapy. Multidisciplinary evaluation is critical.
special_situations.management: For isolated local recurrence: consider clinical trial, systemic therapy, or re-resection in highly selected patients after detailed restaging and multimodal therapy plan. For metastatic recurrence: clinical trial preferred or systemic therapy. If recurrence occurs ≥6 months from completion of primary therapy, repeat previous regimen or switch is acceptable. If <6 months, switch drug class is recommended.
special_situations.evidence: Retrospective data suggest potential benefit of re-resection for isolated local recurrence (5-year survival 40.6% in pooled analysis, reference 313). Management of metastatic recurrence is based on extrapolation from systemic therapy trials.

special_situations.key_message: Platinum-based chemotherapy and PARP inhibitors are key therapeutic options for patients with germline or somatic BRCA1/2 or PALB2 mutations.
special_situations.management: For neoadjuvant/adjuvant setting: cisplatin/gemcitabine is an option for known BRCA1/2 or PALB2 mutations. For metastatic disease: platinum-based regimens (e.g., cisplatin/gemcitabine) are recommended. For maintenance therapy after platinum-based chemotherapy with no progression: olaparib (preferred for germline BRCA1/2) or rucaparib (for germline or somatic BRCA1/2/PALB2 mutations).
special_situations.evidence: Phase III POLO trial showed improved PFS with olaparib maintenance vs placebo in germline BRCA-mutated metastatic pancreatic cancer (median PFS 7.4 vs 3.8 months, HR 0.53, P=.004).

special_situations.key_message: Immunotherapy with pembrolizumab is highly effective for tumors with microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) or mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR).
special_situations.management: Pembrolizumab is recommended as first-line or subsequent therapy for patients with MSI-H/dMMR tumors. Dostarlimab-gxly is an option as subsequent therapy if no prior immunotherapy. Ipilimumab plus nivolumab may be considered for TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb) tumors.
special_situations.evidence: Phase II KEYNOTE-158 trial showed an ORR of 34.3% and durable responses in patients with MSI-H/dMMR noncolorectal tumors, including pancreatic cancer.

special_situations.key_message: Targeted therapy with dabrafenib/trametinib is effective for tumors harboring BRAF V600E mutations.
special_situations.management: Dabrafenib/trametinib is recommended as first-line therapy for metastatic disease (category 2A) and as subsequent therapy. It is a category 2B recommendation for first-line metastatic disease with poor PS.
special_situations.evidence: The ROAR basket trial and NCI-MATCH trial demonstrated efficacy and acceptable toxicity for dabrafenib/trametinib in BRAF V600E-mutated solid tumors, including pancreatic cancer.

special_situations.key_message: TRK inhibitors (larotrectinib, entrectinib, repotrectinib) are highly effective for NTRK gene fusion-positive solid tumors.
special_situations.management: Larotrectinib or entrectinib are recommended as first-line and subsequent therapy for NTRK fusion-positive locally advanced or metastatic disease. Repotrectinib is recommended as first-line therapy for PS 0-2 and subsequent therapy for PS 0-1.
special_situations.evidence: Pooled analysis of larotrectinib trials showed an ORR of 79% with durable responses in NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors. Entrectinib showed an ORR of 75% in integrated analysis of three trials.

special_situations.situation: HER2 Overexpression (IHC3+ or IHC2+ with FISH HER2 amplified)
special_situations.key_message: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is an option for patients with HER2-overexpressing solid tumors after prior systemic therapy.
special_situations.management: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki is recommended as subsequent therapy for patients with good PS and HER2-positive tumors.
special_situations.evidence: DESTINY-PanTumor02 phase II trial showed antitumor activity in patients with HER2-expressing solid tumors, including pancreatic cancer.

clinical_pearls: Multidisciplinary consultation at a high-volume center is essential for optimal management of all pancreatic cancer patients, impacting staging, surgical candidacy, and treatment planning.
High-quality dedicated pancreatic protocol CT (or MRI) is the cornerstone for staging and resectability assessment. The use of a standardized radiology reporting template is recommended.

CA 19-9 is a useful biomarker but must be interpreted with caution. It can be elevated due to biliary obstruction, cholangitis, or inflammation, and is undetectable in Lewis antigen-negative individuals. Best measured after biliary decompression.
For borderline resectable disease, neoadjuvant therapy is now the standard of care approach at most NCCN member institutions, with upfront surgery no longer recommended.
Resection should be performed at high-volume centers (≥15-20 pancreatic resections annually) to optimize surgical outcomes, including lower perioperative mortality and higher R0 resection rates.

Molecular profiling using next-generation sequencing (NGS) is recommended for all patients with advanced disease who are candidates for anti-cancer therapy, to identify actionable alterations (e.g., BRCA1/2, MSI-H, NTRK fusion, BRAF V600E).
Immunotherapy is highly effective for the small subset of pancreatic cancers with MSI-H/dMMR tumors, making molecular testing imperative.
Germline BRCA1/2 testing is recommended for all patients with confirmed pancreatic cancer, as it has implications for treatment (platinum sensitivity, PARP inhibition) and for familial screening.

Supportive care is integral to management, including biliary stenting for obstruction, celiac plexus neurolysis for pain, pancreatic enzyme replacement for exocrine insufficiency, and nutritional support.
The role of ctDNA for minimal residual disease detection is emerging but not yet standard for guiding adjuvant therapy decisions.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma
guidelines_resources.notes: Version 2.2026. Provides evidence-based recommendations for diagnosis, staging, treatment, and supportive care. Available at www.nccn.org.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides criteria for genetic testing and management of hereditary cancer syndromes, including pancreatic cancer susceptibility genes.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities
guidelines_resources.notes: Essential for managing immune-related adverse events from pembrolizumab and other checkpoint inhibitors.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides framework for palliative and supportive care throughout the disease trajectory.

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition
guidelines_resources.notes: Defines TNM staging for pancreatic adenocarcinoma, used for prognosis and treatment planning.

guidelines_resources.title: Cancer Protocol Template for Carcinoma of the Pancreas
guidelines_resources.notes: Standardized pathology synoptic reports for pancreatic cancer specimens.

key_trials.key_result: Median OS 11.1 vs 6.8 months (HR 0.57, P<0.001); Median PFS 6.4 vs 3.3 months (P<0.001).
key_trials.practice_change: Established FOLFIRINOX as a preferred first-line regimen for metastatic pancreatic cancer in good PS patients.

key_trials.secondary_outcomes: ORR 23% vs 7%; Median PFS 5.5 vs 3.7 months.
key_trials.practice_change: Established albumin-bound paclitaxel/gemcitabine as a category 1 preferred first-line regimen for metastatic disease.

key_trials.full_name: European Study Group for Pancreatic Cancer - 4
key_trials.population: Resected pancreatic cancer, no prior neoadjuvant therapy
key_trials.practice_change: Established capecitabine/gemcitabine as a preferred adjuvant regimen for resected pancreatic cancer (category 1).

key_trials.key_result: Median DFS 21.6 vs 12.8 months (HR 0.58, P<0.001); Median OS 53.5 vs 35.5 months (HR 0.64, P=0.003).
key_trials.practice_change: Established modified FOLFIRINOX as a preferred adjuvant regimen for resected pancreatic cancer in good PS patients (category 1).

key_trials.population: Resected pancreatic cancer, no prior chemotherapy/RT
key_trials.key_result: Median DFS 13.4 vs 6.9 months (P<0.001); Median OS 22.8 vs 20.2 months (HR 0.76, P=0.01).
key_trials.practice_change: Established adjuvant gemcitabine as a standard of care for resected pancreatic cancer.
Established NALIRIFOX as a category 1 preferred first-line regimen for metastatic disease, with the caveat that it does not appear to have an advantage over FOLFIRINOX.

key_trials.full_name: Olaparib Maintenance Therapy for BRCA-Mutated Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer
key_trials.population: Metastatic pancreatic cancer with germline BRCA1/2 mutation, no progression after ≥16 weeks of platinum-based first-line chemotherapy
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS 7.4 vs 3.8 months (HR 0.53, 95% CI 0.35-0.82, P=0.004).
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: No significant OS difference at interim analysis (18.9 vs 16.1 months, HR 0.91, P=0.68).
key_trials.practice_change: Established olaparib as a preferred maintenance therapy for germline BRCA1/2-mutated metastatic pancreatic cancer after platinum-based first-line therapy.

key_trials.population: Locally advanced pancreatic cancer controlled after 4 months of gemcitabine ± erlotinib
key_trials.key_result: No significant OS difference (HR 1.03, 95% CI 0.79-1.34, P=0.83).
key_trials.practice_change: Based on trial data, there is no clear survival benefit with addition of conventional chemoradiation following gemcitabine monotherapy in locally advanced disease. Chemoradiation may improve local control and delay need for resumption therapy.
